comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hiya, John – I’ve got an actual weird circumstance at work…

You might recall that I drive a forklift generally regarded as hard to operate, and thus nicknamed the Yellow Monster. I asked you what’s-going-on-here-??? when my co-workers started calling me the Yellow Monster. Apparently, I’ve gotten conflated with the egregore of a machine that can maneuver where others can’t by driving a machine that others won’t. OK, fine, deal. Funny. Ha.

Well, there’s more. Recently, one of the boys declared that my theme song is “I am the Warlock” by Probot, featuring Jack Black. (NSFW, btw.) Nothing like a pretty good parody metal song about a B-reel horror flick, right? Why that song? “Because,” he intoned, “you are the Warlock.” Err… only a warlock can ride the Yellow Monster? That question started an ongoing heated debate over what to call said forklift when I drive it: Warlock 1 or the Yellow Monster.

Thing is, hardly anyone outside my family and closest friends know that I am a Druid, and maybe three people know that I’m working on The Dolmen Arch. Yet, I’ve been assigned magical powers by people who are uninterested in the extramundane. So… given the instant popularity of my theme song, and given that the song issuer doesn’t know about my activities, how do they know? Why is my assigned persona an opposite of who I am and what I’m up to? What’s-going-on-here-???

Rhydlyd

comments.title: Healings and Sphere of Light
comments.content:
Good Evening/Morning *insert unpronounceable Welsh name here*

1) You mentioned that miraculous healings were a relatively common side effect of the mystical path. Is that because the deity you are aligning yourself with is able to kind of reach through the mystic to make the change or because something happens to the mystic through the aligning process that allows a resonance of some kind to flow between mystic and the previously unwell one or … ? How does this work?

2) In the Sphere of Light part of the process is that you are consciously aligning your will with the will of God with an attitude of love and loyalty – it also mentions that you can do it morning and evening and eventually anytime through the day you sense/feel the need for it. I have been practicing this but instead of releasing it after the morning one I have been leaving it there kind of like a dimmer switch version so instead of a bright sphere of light it is like a opalescent glow that I am thinking of as a spiritual armour … and regularly through the day I am aware of it and thus re-aligning myself in terms of my will/His will. That would seem a happy side effect in any case but is that the way it is supposed to work/be done?

Thank you

replies.title: Re: Healings and Sphere of Light
replies.content:
1) I'm not a mystic, and so I'm really not sure how it works. What I know is that mystics in every religious tradition I've ever heard of have this capacity to pray for sick people and, tolerably often, cause anything from a mild improvement to a sudden and kincomprehensible recovery from a fatal ailment. For what it's worth, the mystics always say "It's not me, it's the god," so you can treat that as a data point as well.

2) I know of no reason why you shouldn't do that.

replies.title: Re: Healings and Sphere of Light
replies.content: 1) Which sounds very different from the way things like reiki describe themselves as working.  Are there many methodologies of healing you are aware of that don't require herbs/substances etc and is there a resource you could recommend to look up what they are about (if I was to go onto Wikipedia and type in say Homeopathy or Cell Salts you can imagine the oh so helpful data I would find there)

replies.title: Re: Healings and Sphere of Light
replies.content: Well, there are dozens if not hundreds of healing methodologies; do a search on DuckDuckGo for "alternative healing" and you'll get plenty of leads. Most mystics don't tend to use those -- they just pray, and let their god do what gods do -- but there are exceptions.

replies.title: Re: Healings and Sphere of Light
replies.content:
I can vouch for this as well. Sadhguru's latest book, Karma, talks a bit about this.

He uses completely different definitions for Yogi, Saint, Mystic than the Western Occult tradition outline.

Yogi - someone who drops the experiential/existence barriers to the Ultimate so that everything is either experienced as "me" or experienced as "there's no 'me' anywhere." That is, the energy-body/mental-body/physical body operations of "this-and-that" of everyday life ceases momentarily. For a while you set aside the tiny body/mind-identity you've accumulated since birth to experience the possibilities of life beyond that heap of accumulation. It gives you a real taste of seeing what each person on this planet really is and is capable of. To Mother Nature there is no "you and me". It's all just one big Tatwa getting expressed in an infinite number of ways. A Yogi experiences that as their everyday life.


Saint - this is typically what Western Traditions call Mystic. A Saint only mostly knows how to align his/her own consciousness with Higher Superconsciousness and then walks around with a D&D kind of Paladin blessing aura or they deliberately activate their consciousness to align with a higher spiritual Plane being to transfer blessings to someone else. The kicker is that the saint doesn't have a lot in their toolbox to teach to someone else other than praying or chanting or maybe a few simple things. Despite the fact they don't bother having a lot of methodologies the world becomes a much nicer place typically when a lot of these kind of people begin to show up. Unfortunately the current era seems to have a somewhat critical shortage of these people right now so every little bit a spiritual aspirant can emulate them helps both the aspirant and everyone around them.


Mystic - this is what someone like Patanjali of the Yoga Sutras fame was. Or Agastyamuni, creator of the original Hatha Yoga. The difference is the Mystic has actual DIY technologies, techniques, kriyas or rituals to give to a spiritual aspirant so the aspirant can evolve themselves upward. They can do things that seem miraculous to the people around them - hence the term Mystic because ordinary people are mystified as to how they're doing what they're doing. They know the possibilities of all the various bodies humans incarnate on Earth with and can teach you how to blossom yourself spiritually upward with a universe-load of different techniques customized to your body-mind pattern for this incarnation. They're the engineers/scientists of the spiritual path. Longest and hardest to evolve yourself toward but very worth while.

comments.title: Godspouses?
comments.content: I recently came across a new term, or practice, or something.  I've found more than a few persons who call themselves "godspouses," and claim to have marriages to Deities.  Mostly, I've encountered folks who claim to be married to Loki or Odin, though I've seen other Deities mentioned, too.  I'm not sure what to make of this, but I do recall in some of the myths and lore, that Gods have coupled with humans, and I also seem to recall something about the lore having human-faery marriages.  Do you know much about this modern practice of marriages to non-human beings?  Some of what I saw seemed very much like "fangirl" stuff but I've encountered folks who seem quite serious about this.  I also welcome any insights from the commentariat, too.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
I know it's something that happens. I'm far from sure the people in question are actually consorting with deities -- not least because there's an equivalent phenomenon of "Snapewives," women (well, mostly) who are convinced that they're astrally wedded to the character Septimus Snape from those tacky films, and a very large number of the Loki and Odin "wives" are "married" to the characters in the movies. Incubi and succubi are of course quite real, and I'm quite sure they're capable of dressing up as characters from movies if they have a mind to.

One thing to keep in mind when comparing the current phenomenon to the legends is that when deities have sex with mortals, inevitably, nine months later there's a baby. Zeus couldn't even, er, spill his seed upon the ground without impregnating the Earth and producing a child! I'll be a little less skeptical of the claims of "godspouses" when they get pregnant and give birth to children who have supernatural strength or wisdom...

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
I very much wonder how widespread the modern godspouse phenomenon is; if it's predominantly North American then another possibility is that we might be looking at an unusually sexual manifestation of the relationship with the tamanous. (IIRC Violet has also speculated along compatible lines about the tamanous relationship, though not on the godspouse phenomenon directly.) Whether it's a *wise* manifestation is another question entirely, but I suspect we would be be supposed to figure that out on our own even if we hadn't treated the previous inhabitants so horrendously.

(There may also be older precedent outside of the realm of legends even in other parts of the world; one of favorite songs to this day is "The Mystic's Dream", which IIRC in classic Lorenna McKinnett fashion is an old work put to song - in this case a Christian monk's or nun's writing about an encounter with a Jesii that sure sounds like it was rather... erotic in nature. That admittedly could have been an incubus encounter, but I'd guess it was something else - not necessarily divine, but not demonic either.)

- pretentious_username

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: In a similar vein, “In Your Eyes” by Peter Gabriel comes to mind because it uses a poetic tradition that deliberately blurs the distinction between the poet’s beloved and the Queen of Heaven.  That angle might be worth considering.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: The tamanous is certainly an interesting angle from which to consider this.  I've begun to suspect that many North Americans are encountering their particular tamanous under the name of known Deities, such as Jesus, Odin, Venus, etc.  That in part explains (at least to my mind) why I can hear two worshippers speak about their experiences of the same Deity, yet the Deity being described often doesn't sound like the same divine person.  It also helps to explain why, as jprussel asked, Gods would enter into ongoing marriages with humans.  If the tamanous are spirits or lesser divinities that form lifelong bonds with individual humans, it might not be so odd.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
Yeah, I also suspect that's the case, or at least something close to it is. My standing interpretative framework is that there are divine archetypes and that individual deities are specific manifestations of such archetypes (AIUI this is also roughly what the Greeks and Romans believed, hence the Interpretatio Graecia); I could easily see the tamanous who takes the name of a known deity being a specific manifestation of an archetype, or of a deity via the same process running one or more levels down. (I think North America also tends to a view of the world in general that blurs the distinction between human and divine a lot more than usual; a characteristic I've noticed in quite a few works that feel tamanous-inspired (both old myths and more recent stories) is a tendency to humanize the mythic, and perhaps also to mythicize the human.

For that matter, the tamanous appearing as a fictional character *also* would not exactly surprise me; the line between a character and a spirit or deity has always felt thin to me.

- pretentious_username

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: Yeah, that does make a good deal of sense, at least to me.  The possibility that someone's tamanous might appear to them as a fictional character that they have a deep connection to is an interesting one.  The divine condescends to meet humans where they are, at least in North America (though I suspect elsewhere, too).  A lot to meditate on, anyway.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
I agree. My guiding spirit seems to be a Roman, very civic-oriented goddess - Minerva, Magistra.

On the other hand, what are the tribal myths of the Western Civilization? The Founders deliberately drew very heavily on the Roman Republic. OTH, every pioneer had a copy of the King James Bible and something by Shakespeare, according to de Toqueville. The design of the usual county courthouse, before the replacement of beautiful old buildings with concrete, glass, and steel boxes,seem to echo Greece and Rome, but with that central rotunda-and-dome, are no such thing. So - you tell me?

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: Karen McCarthy Brown write about something of the sort in her book Mama Lola: A Vodou Priestess in Brooklyn (1991). "Mama Lola" (a pseudonym) was a real mambo from Haiti, living in Brooklyn. One part of of her practice was to reserve one night each week for one of her orishas; that night she slept apart from her husband, to be more readily available for the orisha. (I know a modern Pagan who very quietly does something similar for one of her divine Patrons, a Greek God, though in her case there is no human spouse with whom she had to negotiate the practice.)

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: And Zora Neale Hurston writes in Tell My Horse (1938) about men in Haiti devoted to Erzulie Freida. There's a lengthy section in Chapter 10. She writes that if the men are single at the time they become dedicated to her, they can never marry; if they are already married, they nonetheless spend some nights entirely apart from their wives, in a bed reserved strictly for the man and the goddess. All I know about it is what I've read, but it's a remarkable account.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: I heard of the book and the practice but again aspects of it are still around the community in some ways (in my opinion it should be let go)

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
I will have to do some digging, but I do remember reading about a tradition of an Asian tribe where holy women married spirits.

Far closer to us is the Vodoun tradition of marrying the Loa, which is where my guess the godspouse tradition in modern neo-paganism springs from. The marriage is considered an actual commitment in Vodoun, and it is dangerous to not live up to your end of the bargain. Time must be set aside each week in devotion to your Loa spouse, and that can include sexual activities.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: I'm beginning to think that Lwa marriages might very well be a good starting point for learning more about this newer phenomenon of godspouses.  There does seem to be some similarities here.  Perhaps a key difference is that, presumably, in Vodou there are safeguards in place that would prevent an unsuspecting person from marrying a malevolent being posing as a Lwa.  I'm not sure that can be said of Neo-Pagans.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?

Rhydlyd

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
Not sure if this is much help or not, but something related I've encountered is the idea of "God thralls", which I've mostly seen discussed in the books I've ready by Galina Krasskova and Raven Kaldera (A Modern Guide to Heathenry and Northern Tradition for the Solitary Practitioner). Here, rather than "marriages", folks describe being "owned" by the deity in question. Again, Odin and Loki seem like common entities cited, though there seems to be less reliance on the Marvel versions than JMG mentions in his comment. I haven't met or talked to any of these folks, but they seem to have (or at least to describe) rather intensive devotional practices toward the God they "belong" to.

As for marriages, well, I can certainly give some credence to spiritually significant encounters that include an astrally erotic component, but ongoing marriage? That seems a bit odd for an entity of far greater scope than a human with responsibility for all humans (at least).

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: I also encountered that, Godslaves or thralls.  Interestingly enough, Galina Krasskova is one of the Godspouses that I had in mind as I asked my question.  She claims a marriage to Odin.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
Ah, that's likely what sparked the association, though I had forgotten that! I find much in her writing useful, though that particular piece strikes me as a bit odd. I'm not sure how much of that is hold over from a life going from blandly respectable American Protestant to atheist to fumbling around polytheist, and how much is a genuine response to something weird going on.

What complicates the question for me is that her insights into what the Gods/Goddesses are like in A Guide to Modern Heathenry and some of the recommendations for devotional practice in Northern Tradition for the Solitary Practitioner have been tremendously helpful for me. So, I'm left trying to sort the wheat from the chaff, which seems to be the norm for alternative spirituality.

I'm curious, have you found anything especially valuable from these self-identified "godspouses" to lead you to ask about it?

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: Especially valuable?  No, not really.  I've actually been wondering about humans and intermarriage/interbreeding with non-human beings for a little while.  I've always wondered about the account of the Nephilim in Genesis 6, as just one example.  As I've learned more about the faery and their sometimes sexual interest in human beings, it has led to even more unexpected, though quite interesting, discoveries like this phenomenon of Godspouses.  If the ancient myths and legends are any indication, humans and non-human beings (from gods and angels to much less pleasant spiritual beings) have been procreating and engaging in sexual/romantic/marital unions for a very long time.  I don't know if Godspouses are a modern continuation of that or just fandom on steroids with some malevolent spirits tossed in for good measure.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content: There is a lengthier and quite interesting account of this event in the Book of Enoch (I Enoch, as Biblical scholars call it), which is still one of the canonical books of the Bible for the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church.  It was originally a mainstream Jewish scripture, written in Hebrew, and it is even quoted in one of the books of the Christian Bible (Jude), though the Rabbis eventually excluded it from the Tanakh.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?

For that matter, there is the clear and unambiguous promise in the Scriptures that Christ will marry his bride, which is the Church, at the end of the age.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?

Something else that occurs to me as I reflect on this is the Virgin Birth of Christ. This comes to mind as I think over JMG's comment about these divine-human unions resulting in the birth of divine or semi-divine humans. replies.content: As far as my religion's leaders, I got used to the disconnect between "de jure" and "de facto" a long time ago.  I usually meditate on Matthew 23:2-3.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
In ancient heathenry there is actually a precedent for this, at least in Sweden, and at least with Frey. There is often a young woman who is both priestess and wife to Frey, this is parodied in "Ögmundar þáttr dytts ok Gunnars helmings". Interestingly, priests of Frey are called Frey's "friends", as in "Hrafnkel's Saga."

Whether or not the people claiming to be married to dieties these days, which includes folks like Freya Aswynn and Galina Krasskova, as well as teenagers who imagine Loki more as the Marvel character, is legit, I don't really know.

replies.title: Re: Godspouses?
replies.content:
I knew several such people back in the day - way back in the day, prior to the Marvel movies, when Odin and Loki had to get wives on the basis of their mythological character alone with no help from Tom Hiddleston. There were a very small handful that, I think, had something genuine going on. It was something akin to being a nun (minus any celibacy vows), a very intense devotional, mystical relationship that may not have had any erotic element at all (or at least it wasn't something they ever talked about).

The great majority of them though, had very vivid imaginations, read too much fantasy, and had miserable lives. That crowd had so much chronic illness, mental illness, childhood abuse, eternal social misfits, etc. The god marriages often seemed like a coping mechanism, a way to find some sense of meaning and purpose in lives that had never gone well for reasons they didn't understand, or to find some sense of hope. I've noticed most of these have long since switched their allegiance to politics and find their meaning in an intense identification with the oppressed class and now place their hope in Democratic politicians.

I'd made the assumption that latter group wasn't in contact with anything outside of their own heads, the idea of malevolent spirits never entered my mind. Now that it has, that's very depressing.

comments.title: Subtle Senses & Divination, Profundity & Discursive Meditation, Good Dead Authors, Reflection
comments.content:
Good evening and thanks very much for all you do here and elsewhere.

I just received my copy of the new edition of The Wealth of Nature and am finding it both enjoyable and extremely thought-provoking.

On to the occult! As always, I welcome thoughts from the commentariat as well.

1. When you perform divination, do you pay any attention to feeling nwyfre or other subtle senses? For an example of what I mean, my ogham are a deck of cards, and while I'm shuffling them focusing on the question I'm asking, I sometimes notice a tingly feeling on the back of my neck and interpret that as the time to stop and draw. Is that sort of thing helpful, distracting, or very much on a person-by-person basis?

2. Another finer point of practice on one of the three legs of the magical tripod: when meditating discursively, I have occasionally gotten glimpses/vague impressions that I am on the edge of some vast, profound understanding wider than what I thought I was meditating on, but is just beyond my grasp. Is it generally best to try to charge right back at those glimpses in future sessions, or to keep on with a regular sequence of themes and let such glimpses reveal themselves as they will?

3. You describe your own reading habits as something like "reading things written by people that are dead". Besides the bibliographies of your own fine books and articles, any recommendations on how to find worthwhile books by dead folks to read?

4. I finally made it through the preliminary lessons of the OSA and have begun the Law of Acceptance work. In your accompanying essay, you mention that the practice of reflection goes back 2500 years, and I was curious what the origin was - the stoic practice of thinking back on the day?

5. Also related to reflection, a challenge I ran into when I first tried it a couple years back after reading Apprenticed to Magic: how much of a problem is it if you find the exercise hopelessly soporific? Should I make a point of doing it before I get in bed to try to cover the whole day without falling asleep, or is more "I guess you needed sleep more than you needed the reflection today. Keep at it and do as much as you can"?

replies.title: Re: Subtle Senses & Divination, Profundity & Discursive Meditation, Good Dead Authors, Reflection
replies.content:
1) Very much on a person-by-person basis.

2) Just keep on with your sequence of themes and let the insights show up when they get around to it. That can be a source of distraction!

3) If your local library doesn't purge its collection, that's a good place to start. I also like doing subject searches on archive.org.

4) The Stoics got it from Pythagoras, who apparently invented it.

5) Let yourself go to sleep. It's actually a good thing, because your subconscious will keep processing the memories all night.

replies.title: Re: Subtle Senses & Divination, Profundity & Discursive Meditation, Good Dead Authors, Reflection
replies.content:
On your question number 3, if you don't mind electronic copies of books, I recommend both Wikisource and Project Gutenberg for public domain books (in the US, this means ones that were published before January 1, 1926). If you want hard copies and can't get them through interlibrary loan (and can afford to pay for them) then try Abe books.
I hope this helps!
-- Chronojourner

replies.content: Thanks very much!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
To JMG and all here,

How does one develop the mental strength to withstand whatever they need to face in the long descent and the wars that are almost certainly in our future? How can one lose their fear of physical pain and violence, of dying a brutal death or what have you. Are there any magic rituals or so on that can be used to strengthen ourselves in order to face the very worst if needed? I don't care if I have to starve or what have you (as this feels a 'natural' way to go and comes from within my body) and I'm not afraid of death, it's the violence and the pain I might feel in the process of dying (dying from something external) that I'm so fearful of.

Logically, I can understand that all of these things have been apart of human lives for as long as humans have been around and that humans have adapted and coped and I've probably been through alot of this in past lives. I know all this. But I can't move past my fear given the priveleged way I grew up (and the myths I grew up around) and I feel so paralysed.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's an extremely difficult question, of course. One thing that can help is taking up a fighting art of some kind. When you're used to sparring with a partner and taking the occasional fist in the face, it may be easier to deal with more extreme forms of violence. Ultimately, though, we're all going to have to face this sort of thing and see how well we do.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
When dealing with questions like this, the first thing you need to figure out is how far you want to go. Ceasing to be a coward is within everyone's reach, becoming a soldier probably isn't. JMG's advice to take up a fighting art is sound. Mental strength and overcoming the fear of pain and violence, in my mind, are two completely different things. Mental strength can be used to deal with fear, and overcoming fear can be used to develop mental strength, but these are discrete traits.

If you are interested in using magic devotions to Mars would be a classic, if risky, technique. Safer and more steady is meditation by drawing themes from whatever system of fighting you study. It isn't enough to understand how to fight, you must seek to understand the nature of conflict itself. Another subject for meditation is to try to adapt the technique to examining the body and its functioning. Finally, take breath control seriously. More than one martial arts school has secret techniques that are nothing more than sophisticated applications of breathing exercises familiar to many occultists.

It may be helpful for you to spend some time examining why you fear injury and violence but not death or starvation; all involve the decidedly uncomfortable loss of bodily integrity. Lot's of people think they aren't afraid of something until they actually encounter it. Indifference to death is both a powerful tool and a dangerous delusion.

As far as direct methods go, people don't really get less afraid, they become more courageous and more accepting of uncomfortable stimuli. More familiarly, as with all forms of magic, focus on what you want not what you don't. What you probably want when you are in a bad situation is to preserve your integrity, or the cohesion of the underlying structure. Whether that integrity is of your own body and mental state, or something more distant like somebody else's health or a social order, is simply a question of focus and intention.

As an example: your body functions in a certain fashion. If you suffer a gunshot, it functions differently, not as well of course, but it isn't completely disabled. How do you use your remaining faculties? Neutralize the assailant? Try to treat your injury? Accept that what you are doing is more important than your life and keep working on something else? These calculations become reflexive once you are properly grounded; and they can only be performed if you are focusing on what you want.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm from a very wealthy, sheltered background myself, and have struggled with this as well. I think I'm finally getting the hang of it, but it's not easy. So, before I give my advice, I just want you to know this is possible, and as you do it, door after door will open for you.

a) One very, very useful trick is what the stoics call voluntary discomfort. Basically the idea is that you intentionally push yourself out of your comfort zone, so as to strengthen your will and focus. This is useful because as you train your will and focus, it becomes easier to apply it elsewhere. I'm not suggesting you intentionally put yourself at risk, but if there are things you want to do that involve enough risk you'd usually choose not to, take the chance, and gradually you'll get more and more comfortable with it.

b) One other thing is that it works really well to tackle the myths head on. They shape your thinking in ways you don't even notice, but all link together in such a way that tackling one of them will make it easier to break all of them. My advice here is to intentionally pick some piece of technology which annoys you, which you dislike, or which you find too expensive, and get rid of it. Make whatever adjustments you need, and then repeat. As you free your life from the technosphere, you'll find it easier and easier to break the myths holding you back.

c) Get comfortable with guns. Learn gun safety, how to shoot, how to clean and maintain them. One major issue a lot of people from comfortable backgrounds have is that weapons have been systematically excluded from our worlds; and so introducing them back in controlled manners will make it easier to cope when they are reintroduced in an uncontrolled fashion.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Anonymous here has some really great points! A) and C) especially resonate for me - I was a nerdy, bookish classics major, and one of the reasons I joined the Army after college was to push myself to get comfortable with things that were totally outside of my experience. Obviously going to vary from person to person, but to build on the thoughts above:

1) It can be helpful to find relatively safe ways to stimulate feelings of fear for physical safety and voluntarily expose yourself to them. Practicing a martial art where you get punched, traversing obstacle courses/doing rock climbing, and skydiving all come to mind. Learning what it feels like to be really scared to do something, and then to do it anyway, is fairly transferrable. Also, as I discovered at the top of a ~3 story ladder obstacle, it is strangely empowering to realize that the only thing between you and injury is your own strength and skill and to walk away unharmed.

2) I would second anonymous's recommendation in c) to get comfortable with guns (or some other deadly weapon). There is certainly a lot of nonsense around gun culture and anti-gun culture, especially in the US, but what I think is most useful about them is they force you to confront an inherent reality: you are always carrying around deadly weapons (your hands and teeth, if nothing else). Guns externalize and make extremely clear this fact, and come with a set of rules for being safe/intentional with that deadly potential. It's kinda weird, but when you handle guns enough, you simultaneously realize that they are "just a tool" that isn't that big a deal, but also a weighty responsibility you have to take absolutely seriously. This essay by Eric S. Raymond on the ethical and moral implications of bearing arms shaped a lot of my thinking here: http://catb.org/~esr/guns/gun-ethics.html

3) For a more intellectual take on things, I found the books On Killing and On Combat by Lieutenant Colonel Dave Grossman extremely helpful for thinking about violence, our relationship to it, and how to integrate a capacity for violence into a healthy self-image. What especially called these to mind was your comment about being more fearful of violence than of the actual death. The research seems to indicate that we are far more adversely psychologically affected by other people intending us harm than we are by the harm itself - way more folks get PTSD from break-ins and muggings than from arguably more damaging natural disasters because it's the malevolence of humans that is so affecting.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I did four years of a particularly intense martial art. It was interesting to note that for the majority of people getting into a ring and hitting someone was actually much more difficult then getting hit. You got hit and you were surprised/startled but almost immediately some part of your mind kicked in with a stern "let's not let that happen again."

But hitting someone... really hitting them, made a lot of people deeply uneasy. It flew counter to what they had been taught about what a good/decent/moral person did. And it was scary. What if you hit someone and they seemed unaffected? Well, that is the stuff of nightmares. But what if you clipped someone on the jaw and they went down like a sack of bricks? For many people that is a different kind of fear. The solution for many people was to just not hit someone. It became the classic line of the teacher, "stop trying to hit me, and hit me!"

AV

replies.content: I played high school football, even though I've never especially liked sports.  The rough contact changed me, gave me a sort of confidence I hadn't experienced before.  If I had it to do over, and if martial arts training were available where I grew up, I'd do that instead.  Guns?  Yeah, I agree with what's been said.  One of my friends, in his 60s, was and is completely unfamiliar with guns; he found a loaded revolver in a relative's belongings after they had died, and he didn't even know how to unload it. What he did with it makes an amusing and scary story.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I took up Muay Thai boxing going 2-3 times a week for 5 years and our work was done with partners. Sparring was weekly. It was invaluable for me to know what pain felt like from working out, seeing how others respond and deal with pain, doing the same repetitive practices and seeing incremental improvements, and the most important, getting punched and staying in the fight.

Occasionally someone would start and their energy would be completely aggressive and they felt they had to dominate everyone and every exercise. Those people typically lasted less than a month. 95% of Muay Thai boxers are completely chill and work on themselves and their mental game.

It also helps to look at the lives of people even only 100 years ago pre antibiotics and pre most modern conveniences. And keep a journal for yourself of what you are thinking and feeling about current events. Historians will love you for it.

comments.title: Beginning Astrology
comments.content: I was wondering if you had any thoughts or recommendations on getting started with Astrology? I've gone back and forth whether or not to try it over the years; and I finally made a casual attempt at casting a natal chart with salutary results. Besides book recommendations, do you have any advice on how to practice or become more skilled?

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content: Quite a few people have recommended Parker's Astrology by Derek and Julia Parker as a good beginner's text on the subject. Close study of your own natal chart is one of the classic ways to go further -- once you've worked out what every placement and aspect means, you've got some sense of how a horoscope works -- and casting many, many other charts is an essential next step. I've also learned a great deal by tracking planetary transits across my natal chart, and comparing that to the events that happen during the transit.

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content: Another thing I was curious about; do you know if anybody has created a full tradition of ritual magic (like the golden dawn) but rooted in astrology rather than cabalistic philosophy? If not, is there some reason why they haven't? It seems to me that both anatomy and classical philosophy map onto the cosmos more easily than onto the tree of life.

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content: Not that I know of. Curiously enough, though, that's a project I've been considering. It will be a whale of a lot of work, and I've got some other projects to get out of the way first, but it's certainly an option. I suspect the only reason there isn't one yet is because it would take the aforementioned whale of a lot of work.

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content:
Well, I have to say that project sounds fascinating...

Anyway those of us interested could help you with the whale of a lot of work?

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content:
Discursive meditation and pathworking on the planets, signs, houses, and aspects will be the first step in the work, and yes, that's something that others can do as well. Learning as much as possible about classic natural magic and astrological magic would also be very helpful.

My thought is that any such project shouldn't simply be a Golden Dawn variant gimmicked to fit astrological symbolism -- that's certainly an option, to be fair, but it's not one that interests me. There's already a very rich heritage of astrological magic to use as a springboard, including both natural magic and the sort of work found in the Picatrix (what Agrippa calls "mathematical magic"). The question is simply how to add the third, ceremonial level, the level that isn't dependent on matter (natural magic) or time (mathematical magic) but reaches directly up to the eternal archetypes of the heavenly forces. Of course the other two should also be included in the course of study; it might work well to have three degrees of initiation, the first focusing on natural magic, the second focusing on classic astrological magic, and the third focusing on ceremonial magic.

But all this is tentative, and subject to modification on the basis of study and practice.

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content:
In my mind; time and matter (as well as space if you want to rope in sacred geometry) are all functions of a deeper underlying order. I always understood ritual as a systematic attempt at aligning the structure of the self with the structure of the world. The difficulty of course is that humans just aren't developed enough to align with everything at the same time, so priorties must be set and trade-offs made. Extending from that, we have different rituals to work with different energies/patterns.

My instinct would be to teach meditation as a function of the elements, then add workings to deal with elemental energies, then planetary while trying to connect the elements with the zodiac, then finally zodiacal energies. Communion with the zodiac (literally "animals" but more liberally "divine life") could be understood as the symbolic goal of the system.

Natural magic and older energetic anatomy would seem to thread through each of those four stages. If I knew more about sacred geometry, that would probably be of use as well. There is a really obvious correspondance between planetary aspects and joint alignments in martial arts; I would assume that the connection is properly part of sacred geometry.

Do you have any thoughts on this, or am I just getting ahead of myself?

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content: Interesting. I haven't begun the meditations yet that will give me the framework for the system, so I'm not at all sure what else to say in response to this.

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content:
This feels like an excellent and supremely worthy project, and one that I am willing to commit to. Do you have any thoughts or recommendations on how to prepare for it? How far should one get in a system before building your own, or do you need to master more than one?

I spent a couple of years practicing GD magic, but the thing that broke me out of it was a strong preference for the circular mandalas used in every other system I looked at. Cabala always felt like a function of the planets to me, not the other way around. More recently, I've been practicing Druidry (DMH) for about 8 months. I like the system, but it doesn't feel like home to me... now I'm starting to think that this does.

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content: In terms of preparation, I can think of two approaches that would be workable. The first is to really focus on astrology, getting a first-rate grasp of astrological theory and practice, and using discursive meditation and pathworking to deepen your experience of the symbols. The second is to master at least one existing system of magic inside and out. Of course you can do both of these, and -- as noted above in my comment to Anonymous -- learning astrologically based natural magic and classic astrological magic a la Picatrix would be a very solid foundation to build from.

replies.title: Re: Beginning Astrology
replies.content:
I've been trying to figure out what I want to do with my life recently and this discussion has come with a really intense sensation of finally seeing how to get home. I think I'm up for both of these approaches.

As always, thank you so much for your time and your insight!

comments.title: Reactivity I & II
comments.content:
Mr. Greer --

It has been a quiet sort of a week, and I have been enjoying The Devil's Mistress while I get through homework.
This week, my questions are twofold.

1) I have taken to walking up along a nature path near our residential area, usually at night, to reach a small park where I may try to observe the stars. Walking there from my dorm room and back, I have begun to notice the unnecessary electric lights more and more, and feel a sense of revulsion for them. They're not just bright, and make it impossible to see the stars, but they are useless! Even if more people were out and about, it's still an excess amount of light, making it brighter than daylight in places! I wish I could smash them to pieces, but I know that would end up with me arrested, and more lights than ever.
Is this sense of revulsion something that comes with increasing awareness/magical practice?

2) I have noticed references in occult writing to Nordic/Celtic/Latin "races", and their different abilities for magic. I am not sure how seriously to take these claims. Furthermore, looking up the history of Bavaria (where my father's family is from), it seems it was an area settled by "the Celts" (tribes not listed). I don't know if that makes me Norse or Celtic, then. This settles into:
- Is the idea that different races have different magical abilities one with merit?
- If so, how to determine whether Bavaria settles into Norse or Celtic?

replies.title: Re: Reactivity I & II
replies.content:
1) One of the consistent features of magical practice is that it tends to shake the practitioner out of the shallow trance that counts for waking consciousness in most human beings, and so a lot of things that seems reasonable when you're in that trance stand out in their full absurdity. Those lights aren't there by accident, though. I think a lot of people are scared of looking up at the stars, and realizing how insignificant human concerns are.

2) That whole business is a crude oversimplification of a much subtler and more specific thing. In countries where there's a lot of Celtic ancestry in the population, you get many family lines that have the Second Sight -- old occult teachings have it that there was selective breeding for that purpose a long time ago, and the traces still remain. Germanic, Slavic, and Italic ethnic lineages have their own sensitivities and bloodlines. In a place like Bavaria, which has been a crossroads for a couple of thousand years, most people have ancestry from a wide range of sources -- Germanic, Celtic, the western end of the Slavic diaspora, Italic from Roman times and then again during the heyday of the Holy Roman Empire, and remnants of certain very ancient ethnic groups that were driven up into the Alps by later waves of migrants and invaders. As a Bavarian, you're not any one "race," which is a good thing -- purebred animals so often tend to be fragile, sickly, and neurotic!

replies.title: Re: Reactivity I & II
replies.content:
1) That would explain why, back when I was little and communed with the Moon and Jupiter more often, I found the nearby parking lot lights especially cold and frightening!

2) This is ALSO very good to know! I'm curious if the Second Sight would relate to what my father has and his mother had, the ability to sense ghosts and when someone is about to die. It seems I have a degree of that as well, although I've had very little practice using it, thankfully.

As usual, thank you so much!

replies.title: Re: Reactivity I & II
replies.content: 2) That's a classic form of familial psychism, and very common in the Celtic countries. You may have more than a little Celtic ancestry.

replies.title: Re: Reactivity I & II
replies.content: When I was a young boy in the 1940s, we lived for about six years on the outskirts of a small town, where there were no streetlights.  Every clear night I could go out in our yard and see thousands of stars in the night sky, like billions of brilliant diamonds of all sizes spilled out on some cosmic jeweler's cloth to pick through.  And the Milky Way was almost blinding in its greater brilliance.  It was one of the most beautiful things I have ever seen in all my life.  I deeply mourn the loss of such a night sky.  Like our host, I think the ongoing destruction of this unearthly beauty by 24/7 electrical lighting is deliberate, a conscious nurturing of a deep, deep sickness that has come to root itself in humanity's very bones.

replies.title: Re: Reactivity I & II
replies.content:
I can recall dark nights as well in my small town (I'm 67). Now it's semi-dark with a perpetual glow emanating off in the distance from the local Walmart, Lowes and Home Depot (soon to be joined by Starbucks!). Only brighter stars are still visible in the sky. On a rainy evening the glow gets reflected back and forth so much I can see the tree line across the street from me and it looks more like early twilight than the dead of night. The sickness of course is the profit motive with its unending mantra of buy,buy,buy 24/7. Our health, both physical and mental, is taking a hit from this perpetual false daylight and I'm sure there's a occult hit there as well, otherwise why all the craziness? It won't be until the economy finally grinds to a crawl that the mad glare will finally start to go out and we can get a good view of the stars again. Hope I live long enough to see it.

JLfromNH/Azure Disoriented Lobster

replies.title: Re: Reactivity I & II
replies.content:
It may well be deliberate.

In her novel 'The Marriages Between Zones 3, 4 and 5', Doris Lessing created the character of a tyrannical king who wouldn't allow his subjects to look up at the surrounding mountains, let alone the heavens.

This pattern is broken by a Queen, who descends from a higher zone.

It may be that this tyrannical king lives in our time, somewhere in Silicon Valley; or maybe even incarnated in Elon Musk, who casts his satellite net into the heavens to further imprison us.

Lock-downs and masks were also perhaps intended to estrange us from a wider and higher reality.

All the best

Xabier

replies.title: Re: Reactivity I & II
replies.content:
I was talking to my Dad about this sort of thing the other day. He mentioned how, during his first time at sea as a cadet in the Royal Fleet Auxiliary in 1962, the ship was crossing the Bay of Biscay at night. There was a film playing on deck which he didn't find particularly interesting, so he leaned back and looked up at the sky. He said it blew his mind. This feeling of utter insignificance in the face of such incomprehensible beauty and vastness. It clearly left a mark. He isn't a particularly religious man, but I think it was going in that direction.

Owain D.

comments.content:
Dear JMG,
Karma is a subject that comes up often here, and I have a question about its operation that I hope your long expertise can help with.
Like many other people, I have had a life in which I feel myself to be working off karma from previous incarnations, through great personal difficulties and unhappiness - there's no need to wallow in the details - including looking after somebody else who was incapable of providing anything in return.
Well, OK, karma, at least for me. But what about the other person? What about all the others? Karmically speaking, where do emotional vampires, insensitive, inadequate or abusive partners, bad or neglectful parents, actually come from? What previous experiences in an earlier life can plausibly be supposed to karmically incline someone to be an emotional vampire, for example? What purpose can possibly be served for that person? Why are there such people?
(Yes I know this sounds a bit like a question about theodicy and the problem of evil, but as you always remind us karma is not judgemental. Hence this question).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Karma isn't the only factor in the equation. There are three factors to be weighed -- destiny, karma, and will. Destiny is where the universe is trying to lead you; it relates to the future. Karma is the consequences of your actions; it relates to the past. Will is what you do here and now; it relates to the present.

Let's take an abusive parent as an example here. How does a soul become an abusive parent? Typically, after several lives in which the soul has chosen to be selfish and cruel, it's reborn to an abusive parent and abused. That's a test of sorts. If the soul uses that experience as an opportunity to learn why being selfish and cruel is a bad thing, and doesn't treat its own children later in that incarnation the same way, it overcomes its karma and goes on to better things. If the soul doesn't learn that lesson, and the person grows up into an abusive parent, more karma gets piled up, and the next life will be worse. Sooner or later the pain gets intense enough that the lesson is learned, but that can take quite a bit.

Your destiny is always pushing you into situations where you can learn the lessons you need to learn. Karma makes those lessons necessary, and difficult. Your will determines whether you learn the lesson or not.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's a subclass of time travel story. Actually, there's two types of story that can fit under the label; one ("stable time loop") is basically just the predestination paradox (events happened because the characters went back in time, which causes the events that caused the characters to go back in time to happen), but the more common use is the "Groundhog Day loop" (named after the movie from back in the day) where a character has to go back in time repeatedly and redo a set of events over and over until they do it right.

(The comparison of the latter to both the way karma functions and to Buddhist conceptions of Hell is screamingly obvious, and Japan has definitely noticed - Japan has had a decent number of stories with such a loop as a major theme or subtheme lately, often quite popular, and two of the really obvious ones have major Buddhist themes[1]. But I get the sense there's more than that going on with the trope, and I'm not sure what.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A lot of the time-loop stories have a ridiculously large helping of PMC magical thinking embedded in them — we, the glorious and self-defined GoodPeople™ can solve every conceivable problem, if we're only given enough chances! That type of time-loop story acts as a barely camouflaged apology for the failure and implosion of everything the PMC class was so foolishly left in charge of. Those stories also offer our troubled betters the panacea of imagining one CAN escape the consequences of one's actions through the deus-ex-machina of the do-over. For the rest of us, their endless do-overs offer no escape, as it's their do-overs that got us trapped in this hall of mirrors in the first place.

The other half of the dichotomy is the time-loop stories which are barely disguised morality plays, warning that eventually you WILL have to live with the consequences of your actions. There's a bit more variety here, as the bumpkins may have warned the overlords not to meddle with time travel, or the overlords may have warned the bumpkins not to dabble in it. There's always a skeptic to represent the moral path not taken, so it's pretty easy to tell early on which class is going to get blamed for their hubris and overreach.

Those two varieties of time-loop narratives can pretty easily be categorized as: there's no problem here that we won't eventually progress out of, versus the horror of our god-like mistakes will bring about an apocalypse. There's definitely magic afoot in this tidal wave of time travelers washing up in the flickers, but, with progress vs. apocalypse being the product they're mostly selling, I think that particular magic is the same degraded thaumaturgy they've been peddling since the original magic lantern shows.

— Christophe

comments.title: Recurrent thoughts

Good day JMG,

I'd like to ask you a couple of questions which I feel could have occult causes.

Question 1 –
Sometimes I’m in my bedroom or elsewhere and have a a thought.
It could be an appointment I have scheduled and cannot miss.
Or it could be something I have to buy, urgently or not.
Then as soon as I move to another room or place I forget it completely.

I then go back to the room I was before, where I had the thought
and suddenly it pops in my mind again!

in the air and my brain picks it up like a radio receiver. A true TSW moment.

Question 2 –
Another thing happens everyday as I go to bed and also
as I wake up but ‘am still lying ’.
Bad thoughts about disease, disaster, lascivious assail me to exhaustion.
It feels like this unpleasant thoughts are hanging in the air just waiting
for me to lie on my bed. This happens ‘only’ in my bedroom.
And it happens ‘only when I am lying’, not when I get up.
I do not perceive or feel entities but I get some relief when I place a

What could this be?

replies.title: Re: Recurrent thoughts
replies.content:
Not JMG, obviously, and I can't speak to #2, but #1 is actually quite common and is called the "Doorway Effect" (or sometimes the "Threshold Effect"). It's actually a "feature" of our brains to compartmentalize our memory according to our locale, and doorways and rooms in buildings trigger this effect quite reliably. What I do to help solve it is that when I realize that it's important, I imagine myself remembering it in the next room (or in the space I need to remember it) then promptly go there and recall it. You can use the term in a search engine to find out more.

There may be other dimensions to this issue, but this one works pretty well for me, in most cases.

Former Orthodox catechumen here who went through an introduction to orthodoxy course under the guidance of a priest.

1.) I never heard any injunction against having altars in bedrooms or any special concerns around the Theotokos or virginal saints. “An icon belongs in every room” is the ideal that was repeated.

2.) The two churches split nearly a thousand years ago, but in Orthodoxy I encountered a persistent and prevailing assumption (tinged with melancholy) that eventually the Roman Catholic church will realize the error its ways and reunite with Orthodoxy. The vibe is that they are errant relatives.

That said, the Orthodox are not permitted (by the Orthodox) to take communion in Roman churches, even though the RC church considers it acceptable and offers it to baptized Orthodox. Only baptized/chrismated Orthodox are permitted communion in Orthodox churches.

"Traditionally, larger icons are often framed with towels. This tradition dates back to antiquity and has a theological basis. According to tradition, an image of the Savior miraculously appeared on a towel during His earthly life to help a suffering man. replies.title: Re: Three questions and a story
replies.content:
1) I tend to avoid anything with a negative prefix, too -- in place of "invulnerable," you could use active verbs and say something like "I rise above all hostile energies -- and transmute them into light and peace." Combine that with intense imagination of that transmuting process and you'll get interesting results. ;-)

2) Depends on what you mean by the third eye. The energy center usually associated with the third eye is the pituitary gland, which is between and above the eyes and a little ways back in the skull. It's also the center associated with clairvoyance and astral seership. That said, the pineal gland has the same structure as a primitive eye; in many reptiles, it is an eye, and in humans it can be influenced by how much light comes through the skull -- thus the differences between religious traditions that shave the top of the head and religious traditions that grow the hair long and have practitioners always wear head coverings.

3) Nope. You made the connection, you have to live with it until it fades out.

4) It sounds to me as though your crystal has been returned to you, cleansed by the elements of nature.

comments.title: Lunar Eclipse November 19th, Victoria, BC - and west coast North America
comments.content:
Hey all,

For those of us concerned with the west coast of North America during these interesting times, I’ve interpreted a chart of the upcoming November 19th lunar eclipse in Victoria, BC. This chart should hold true through Washington and Oregon until the house boundaries change somewhere to the south.

Thank you John Michael Greer for delineating these events, and for providing superb mundane astrology lessons and delineations. I’m avoiding copying material from your delineation here, and I highly recommend folks subscribe for a month or two if able.

Feedback welcome on this chart – I’m struggling to complete this between classes so the delineation is nowhere near exhaustive.

--- Lunar Eclipse, November 19th, in Victoria, BC ---

Lunar eclipse in Taurus, sun in Scorpio. Lunar eclipse in a fixed earth sign indicates sudden shocks to natural resources and/or economies – think of a jolt, shock, or a break in something that has been earthbound or deadlocked. Moon and sun square Jupiter – possible pivot point around industry or large corporations.

In Victoria, Moon in cadent 9th house, sun in cadent 3rd house. This makes me think of something burning deep, rather than ascending.

Malefics in 3rd house can indicate disruptions or troubles in short distance travel, elementary and secondary school education, shared land borders, and a people’s internal sense of cohesion. More lockdowns?

Malefics in 9th house can indicate shocks to long-distance travel, higher education, and people’s sense of collective reach or purpose. More university disruptions, either from lockdowns or from sudden funding cuts.

Moon (representing the politically influential classes) ruling the 11th house of representatives – a government’s representativeness of its people.
Sun (head of state) ruling the 12th house of institutions – a government’s service and/or institutional wrangling of its people.

Eclipses in earth signs combined with malefics (Uranus and Mars here) in Taurus and Scorpio can indicate risk of earthquakes, especially when the eclipse occurs near the Midhaven/Descendent line, or when the eclipse is supported by Jupiter and Saturn. This eclipse is near to but not conjunct with the descendent and midhaven. I see no screaming indications of a quake, but I also get hairs rise on the back of my neck when contemplating this. What if a sudden shock came from somewhere we aren’t expecting?

I’m paying close attention to the 6th house indicating our province’s employment and health. The 6th house here makes my skin crawl.

The ruler of the 6th House is retrograde Uranus in his fall in Taurus, opposing mars ruling Scorpio, and squaring Saturn. Aka work and health during the time governed by this eclipse will be ruled by a malefic “at his worst behaviour” (Greer), opposing a strong and volatile malefic, and squaring a patient and restrictive malefic. Uranus is supported by a sextile with Venus in the 4th, ruling the 2nd and 9th houses. Venusian support from the productive economy and higher ideals, even as health and work prospects crater?

Jupiter is ill-dignified in the 6th house and squares both the Moon and Sun, forming the pivot of the eclipse’s Moon-Sun T-Square. Jupiter (the province’s corporate sector) rules the 4th house of resource industries and agriculture, real estate, and the rural hinterland. It looks like events governed by the eclipse will have a malefic effect on (or be initiated by) work and health issues arising from provincial resource industries ruled by corporations. Too simplistic?

The ruler of the 6th house looks ill-positioned to provide any support.

The ordinary people of the province (1st house) are ruled by Mercury (the intellectual classes), who also rules the 10th house of executive power and authority and is also combust the Sun.

Looking at this chart, I find it gives insight onto power dynamics at play before the eclipse. The intellectual class and talking heads (Mercury) rule both the common people and provincial power, corporations and corporate culture (Jupiter) rule resource industries, and small groups of individuals who have a disproportionate impact on shaping provincial life (Uranus) rule daily health and working conditions. The politically active class (Moon) rule, or think they rule, the branch of government concerned with representing its citizens as a whole, and the sun (provincial head) is a little out of the way on his own mission, most concerned with the functioning of the province’s institutions. I feel a little sorry for the Sun for some reason.

This is what I’ve been able to delineate for the eclipse so far. I hope this helps. Take and give care out there.

Wylie Fuller, aka Coyote Rambler

comments.title: America's Solar Eclipses
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

In your most recent astrology interview you mentioned that a solar eclipse is always a bad omen. This got me thinking about the recent set of two solar eclipses crossing over America, each set apart by seven years, one having completed its run in 2017, the other coming in 2024. I at first thought these formed book ends over an intense period of tribulation. However, I am wondering now if this represents a bad omen that lasts for several years followed by another bad omen that lasts for several more years. Do you have any thoughts on which one might happen? Do the bad omens stop in 2024 or do they last for several more years?

replies.title: Re: America's Solar Eclipses
replies.content: According to traditional astrological lore, they aren't book ends. Each one foretells its own period of trouble, the 2017 eclipse beginning that year and lasting for as many years as the eclipse lasts in hours, the 2024 doing the same thing. So the 2024 eclipse won't be over in 2024 -- the average eclipse lasts 4-5 hours, so that means 2028 or 2029 before that one runs its course.

comments.content:
Well, speaking of what they're teaching kids about history these days - I spent the first 9 years of my life in New Westminster and I didn't know about Moody or the foundation of the city.

I feel Lytton may therefore be somewhat disappointed to know how far his children of "England on the shores of the Pacific" have fallen; "representatives of the best of British culture' who possessed 'courtesy, high breeding and urbane knowledge of the world" we were not... We moved after being broken into and stripped to the walls four times by the same gang in 18 months. Oh wait...! (ha, empire jokes. This actually happened though.)

I had loved our May Day, but hadn't realized it was as unique as it was (only four communities in BC do it), and was quite disappointed when we moved that I wouldn't get to dance the Maypole again, as I'd only just turned old enough the year before.

"New Westminster's May Day celebration began in 1870 and continues today as an important civic tradition, lending the city the distinction of having the longest-running May Day celebration of its type in the British Commonwealth."

"... more formally known as the Hyack Festival, [it] is distinguished by the Ancient and Honourable Hyack Anvil Battery Salute, a tradition created by The New Westminster Fire Department during colonial times as a surrogate for a 21-gun salute. With no cannons available in the early colony, the Fire Department—known as the Hyacks, from the Chinook Jargon for "fast" or "quick", here derived from its use as a command for "hurry up!"— improvised by placing gunpowder between two anvils, the top one upturned, and igniting the charge from a safe distance, hurling the upper anvil into the air.

Each year, in preparation for May Day, local schoolchildren are taught to dance around a maypole with colourful ribbons. Elections are held at elementary schools in the city, and, from them one girl is selected to become the year's May Queen, and two students from each school to become members of her "May Queen Suite" and "Royal Knights." On a Wednesday of the festival, elementary school students gather at Queen's Park Stadium to dance, and the May Queen is crowned."

https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Westminster

I think this may be a magic question, since it's certainly about a major change in consciousness.

I used to be pleased with every bit of recognition I ever got; and I used to love wearing T-shirts that told the world where I stood on some matters, even if it were only cats and books. These days, a banner listing me as a volunteer, or as a good donor for a Village charity, is embarrassing. So are all the lavish thank-you letters.
Also, I seem to have taken for my motto "Look, listen, and be silent." An injunction I would have loudly railed against as recently as 6 months ago! On the other hand, some previously repressed material ha not only come up out my my subconscious, and a very healthy development it is, too, I've managed to untie quite a few emotional knots lately. (I think it becomes easier with practice.)

Is this a result of practicing solitary and really paying attention to the inner work? Or is it some form of burnout?

One thing it may be connected with is my slow departure from the automatic defense of everything Democrats do - did you know that now, even lamenting their tactical errors is starting to be seen as treason to the cause? My daughter is so totally hardcore PMC, not just politically, but complete with all the "We're just ordinary middle-class" and "we're Green - we drive electric cars," but her house leaks energy like a sieve and runs up her utility bills quite a bit, but she likes that house, so... and she goes all Karen on the smallest of "non-virtue signals" such as carrying a reused disposable plastic water bottle. Because it sends her the wrong signal, for all my explanations! (No, I'm not keeping silent out of fear, or even exhaustion. Merely the recognition that it's futile; accept her as she is, as she tries very hard to accept me as I am.)

At any rate, I thought I'd run a reality check by you. I haven't been doing any active magic besides wearing an amulet, but have been doing quite a bit of serious worship.

Thanks,

The Grey Badger.

P.S. One reason she is so edgy and Fundamentalist-PMC-values is that she lives in Florida, and it must feel to her as if she were a fresh water fish swimming in the saltiest and most polluted of right-wing waters. Just as I felt in the corporate environment.

comments.title: Meditating on the Tarot
comments.content:
JMG:

As I continue on my lessons in "Learning Ritual Magic," I am currently meditating on the 5's from the Waite-Ryder-Smith deck. I have a question about whether something I find myself doing is a good idea.

As I have been meditating on the fives, I came up with the keywords "Deprivation" for pentacles, "arrogance" for swords, "loss" for cups, and "strife" for wands. "Arrogance" for swords concerned me because Dion Fortune in "Mystic Qabalah" gives the word "defeat." Certainly, there is a defeat (the two guys walking away are defeated) but the foreground figure is the victor. I read his facial expression as sneering, he is facing headfirst into the wind, and he is carrying more swords (which I take as battle trophies) than he can fight while holding. Thus, he demonstrates arrogance in his victory which will probably bit him in the backside later. That's my read on the card.

After contemplating this, I thought about how the 5 cards related to each other across the four worlds of geburah.

Across the worlds I thought of strife (wands) as the spiritual nature of Geburah - Dion Fortune describes the need for Geburah to clear away the decadence resulting from the operation of Chesed. The strife comes from what stays and what goes as Geburah does its work. One world down (cups) we feel the loss of what is destroyed in the strife. One world down from here (swords), in order to cope with the loss we revel in the victory over what was destroyed in the same way we might use anger to feel better about shame. Regardless, one world down (penatacles) we experience what is lost in the strife as a material deprivation, but also an opening for a spiritual re-evaluation (symbolized by the stained glass window) that can take place in Tipareth.

My question then, looking at the summary above, should I continue to meditate on the minor arcana in a way that considers how they connect across the worlds of the sepheroth (and for that matter how they connect to the other sepheroth or should I lead my thoughts to stick more with the actual card on which I am meditating?

replies.title: Re: Meditating on the Tarot
replies.content:
1) Your titles need not be the same as the Golden Dawn titles (that's what Fortune is quoting).

2) Meditate on whatever works for you -- the four worlds are fine. The goal is to explore the cards in meditation, not to reach some preestablished goal.

On the surface his books, at least the titles, seem somewhat dark and evil.  I have a hunch that this isn't an accurate perception of him (he was a Rosicrucian!) but I don't want to start reading him in the case that it is, at least right now.  Any thoughts on the accuracy or lack of accuracy of this perception would be welcome.  Thanks in advance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The titles of his books are deliberately spooky, but de Guaita's books are very highly regarded in the French occult scene. You might read Oswald Wirth first -- Wirth was a friend and student of de Guaita -- and see if you get on well with his approach; if so, by all means read de Guaita.

comments.title: Question about finding objects
comments.content:
I managed to lose some government ID cards over the weekend, so before going to the government office I tried to locate
them using geomancy.
Here is the chart:
[IMAGE:0]

So based on myself as querent and the 10th house for legal paperwork, I would have expected to turn up in a basement or low ceilinged room (Tristwch), through someone else finding it (Cyswyllt in 12th and 9th houses, by translation). I was unsure what the square of Bendith Fawr was bringing to the picture.

It was found in an office I was at before, given to them by someone who found it outside in the adjacent parking lot. That office is roughly west-south-west of my position.

What things did I miss in the chart?

replies.title: Re: Question about finding objects
replies.content:
Okay, any missing object is 4th house, irrespective of its nature, so the answer is "someone has already found it" -- that's what an occupation generally means in my readings of this kind -- and since Bendith Fawr perfects the chart, there would be at least a small delay.

If you read it as a 10th house question, btw, Tristwch can mean "down on the ground."

comments.content: Are there any herbs or stones-- or other elements of natural magic-- that can be used to facilitate ceremonies of transformation?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Depends on the ceremony -- or more precisely, on what kind of transformation you want to work. An animal body of transformation? You need a feather, a tooth, or some other object link to the creature you want to use as a template for your etheric form. A personal spiritual transformation? You need a stone or other natural substance that reflects the change you want to make. Other examples can be extrapolated from there.

Former Buddhist here:

Alan watts was the real deal. He was formally recognized as a master by the Soto Zen hierarchy back in Japan. comments.title: Great Conjunction and Aires priors 1782
comments.content:
In the recent astrological podcast, it was mentioned that Bonatti recommended checking the Aires ingress prior to the Great Conjunction occurring that year. In light of reading (very slowly) Simon Schama’s Citizens, I began to look into astrological portents of the French Revolution. The 1782 Great Conjunction frames the time period up through the rise of Napoleon. The Aires ingress of that year (March 20, 1782 11:32am Paris) is alarming as it features by traditional astrology an exact square between Saturn in Capricorn (!) and the Sun, with conjunct Jupiter also squaring the Sun from Sagittarius(!!). Venus is conjunct the Sun, but it is also in Mutual reception with Mars(!!!). Uranus was recently discovered, and had only gotten its name some 8 days prior. It’s presence turns this hard Sun-Saturn square into a T-square, the resolving point being somewhere in Libra, which is occupied by unknown and undiscovered Neptune, turning a T-square into a grand cross(!!!!). Neptune being the planet of mass phenomena, this clearly shows the mass armies and mass politics of France during the revolution. Of the remaining heavenly bodies, we have the Moon, Neptune, and Pluto (for what its worth) in a grand trine, with the Moon late in the 11th conjunct the 12th house cusp. The Cancer Ascendent turns out to be the 8th house cusp for the Grand Conjunction chart, Pluto’s position in this Aires ingress turns out to be the Aires ingress 1789 ascendent.

All very spooky stuff, and had me thinking about the Picatrix, and the manuscripts available to English and French occultists. Given how malefic this Aires ingress is, the potential to do great harm for a Grand Conjunction astrological working is greatly magnified. Of the potential operators for such a working, the French had many enemies, the British smarting over the immanent loss of British North America by French aid, it’s neighbors to the east and north. The discovery timeline of Uranus is of note, being initially reported as a comet in Anglo-Hanoverian circles, it’s presence and slow motion is a slow burning fuse that touches royal Leo in 1789 Aires after riling up the political classes in its passage through Cancer in the 7 year interval between 1782 and 1789.

-ighy

replies.title: Re: Great Conjunction and Aries priors 1782
replies.content: Hmm! Very interesting indeed.
